item_name: Dim an LED with a Potentiometer
prompt:
On a clean breadboard, an Arduino is wired to a 5 mm LED and a small rotary potentiometer, with the LED glowing softly. The LED is connected through a current-limiting resistor to one of the Arduino’s PWM-capable pins, while three wires from the potentiometer run back to an analog input and the 5 V and ground rails. A hand gently turns the potentiometer’s knob, and the LED’s brightness is clearly in mid-transition—neither fully bright nor fully off—capturing the idea of adjustable dimming via code.

The scene uses a near-future solarpunk electronics lab aesthetic: soft overhead lighting, pale non-conductive surfaces, and a few neatly arranged sensors and boards in the blurred background. A laptop off to the side shows a stylized graph or slider that echoes the changing brightness, but with no readable text. The 512×512 photorealistic composition centers the Arduino, potentiometer, and LED, with no brand logos, no legible labels, and no visible faces—only hands and forearms gently adjusting the control.
